title: LeakyReLU
description: A ReLU-style activation that keeps a small negative slope instead of zeroing every negative FFN value.
openingSummary: LeakyReLU keeps a small negative slope inside an FFN instead of clamping every negative hidden value to zero, so weak signals and gradients can still pass through the block.

whatItIs.title: What It Is
whatItIs.body: LeakyReLU is a small change to ReLU. Positive hidden values pass through as usual, but negative values are multiplied by a small constant such as 0.01 instead of becoming zero. In a standard FFN after attention, that means a token can keep a weak negative signal rather than shutting the feature off completely.

whatItOptimizes.title: What It Optimizes
whatItOptimizes.body: LeakyReLU optimizes for better negative-side gradient flow while staying very close to ReLU. It keeps the same cheap piecewise-linear behavior, but it avoids a fully dead negative branch.

practicalBenefit.title: Practical Benefit
practicalBenefit.body: For a reader, LeakyReLU is useful because it shows how much behavior can change without redesigning the FFN block. The module still uses one dense path. Only the negative half of the activation rule changes.

howItWorks.title: How It Works
howItWorks.body: The token state expands into a wide hidden layer, then LeakyReLU acts on each hidden value separately. Positive values keep their size. Negative values stay negative, but they are scaled down by a small factor before the block projects back to model width.

mathOrComputeSchema.title: Math Or Compute Schema
mathOrComputeSchema.body: The formula below shows the same split as ReLU, but the negative branch no longer vanishes. Instead, it keeps a small slope alpha.

comparedToNearbyModules.title: Compared To Nearby Modules
comparedToNearbyModules.body: LeakyReLU sits between ReLU and SiLU. It is less abrupt than ReLU because the negative side survives, but it still keeps a piecewise-linear corner rather than moving to the fully smooth SiLU curve.

exampleArchitectures.title: Example Architectures
exampleArchitectures.body: LeakyReLU appears more often in older convolutional or experimental dense networks than in current frontier language models, but it remains a common comparison point when authors discuss negative-side gradient flow.

limitationsAndTradeoffs.title: Limitations And Tradeoffs
limitationsAndTradeoffs.body: LeakyReLU preserves more negative information than ReLU, but the negative side is still fixed and weak. It does not provide the smoother learned filtering that many modern transformer FFNs get from SiLU-based designs.

whyItStillMatters.title: Why It Still Matters
whyItStillMatters.body: LeakyReLU helps readers separate two questions: whether an FFN is dense or gated, and whether its activation fully shuts off negative features. That distinction comes up often in model and paper comparisons.

computeFlow.alt: Activation comparison graph showing how ReLU, LeakyReLU, and SiLU treat negative hidden values inside the same FFN path
computeFlow.caption: LeakyReLU keeps the same dense FFN path as ReLU, but the negative branch now leaks through at a small scale

comparisonTable.caption: How LeakyReLU compares with nearby activation variants

relu.negativeBranch: Clamp every negative hidden value to 0
relu.mainTradeoff: Simplest rule, but fully drops negative signal

leakyRelu.negativeBranch: Keep a small slope alpha x for x < 0
leakyRelu.transitionShape: Hard corner with a weak negative path
leakyRelu.mainTradeoff: Improves negative-side gradient flow, but remains less smooth than SiLU

silu.negativeBranch: Shrink negative values smoothly with x sigma(x)
silu.mainTradeoff: Smoother hidden filtering, but less minimal than a piecewise-linear rule

x.definition: One hidden value inside the expanded FFN state.

negativeBehavior.label: Negative hidden values still flow, but only through a small slope alpha x
